coredata.dc:title: Boosted photocatalytic H2O2 production in pure water with amino-modified N, S-doped carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

Sun, Lei
Zou, Suchang
Bian, Ang
Cui, Yanhua

Li, Chunsheng
Guo, Feng
Shi, Weilong
coredata.dc:description: A simple hydrothermal method was adopted to modify N, S-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) with feathers as precursors and ammonia by amino modification. The as-prepared am-N, S-CDs in amino as in alkaline environments possessed shorter S-N bonds (S2-), which kinetically and effectively increased the rate of electron conduction and reduced the binding of electron-hole pairs. In addition, the amino modification in am-N, S-CDs effectively improved the O2 adsorption capacity, photocatalytic activity and cycling capacity of the catalysts, with the H2O2 yield (384.86 μM) being 3.73 times higher than that of the pristine N, S-CDs (103.12 μM). This study suggests a rational design strategy for chemical bonding to regulate the surface charge transfer of photocatalysts for photochemical-to-chemical energy conversion.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Photocatalyst
Hydrogen peroxide
S-N bonds
Amino modification

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Microstructure and chemical compositions of am-N, S-CDs
Transition of S-N bond in am-N, S-CDs
The effect of S-N bonding on photogenerated electron lifetime

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration for the formation of am-N, S-CDs during the hydrothermal reaction. (b) TEM and HRTEM images and size distribution histograms of am-N, S-CDs. (c) FT-IR spectra of N, S-CDs and am-N, S-CDs. (d) UV–vis absorption spectra of N, S-CDs and am-N, S-CDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of the N, S-CDs: (e) C 1 s, (f) N 1 s and (g) S 2p.

(a) Photocatalytic H2O2 production curves of am-N, S-CDs and N, S-CDs. (b) Photocatalytic H2O2 production of am-N, S-CDs with adding different ammonia content during the synthesis. (c) Solar-to-chemical conversion (SCC) efficiency of am-N, S-CDs and N, S-CDs in water. (d) Photocatalytic decomposition of H2O2 over am-N, S-CDs and N, S-CDs in dark. (e) H2O2 formation (K f) and decomposition (K d) rate constant of with adding am-N, S-CDs and N, S-CDs. (f) A comparison of H2O2 production between as-prepared am-N, S-CDs and other carbon-based photocatalysts in recent years. (g) Photocatalytic H2O2 production over am-N, S-CDs under different scavengers and atmospheres. (h) Selectivity of H2O2 and number of electron transfers (n) corresponding to the applied voltage function, electrochemically measured at a scan rate of 10 mV·s−1 in an O2-saturated electrolyte (ω = 1600). (i) ESR ·O2 – signals of N, S-CDs, am-N, S-CDs-500 and am-N, S-CDs-3500.

(a) Photocatalytic H2O2 production over am-N, S-CDs under different synthetic environment. (b) High-resolution XPS S 2p spectra of N, S-CDs, am-N, S-CDs-500 and am-N, S-CDs-3500. (c) Temperature programmed O2 desorption (TPD-O2) profiles of N, S-CDs and am-N, S-CDs. (d, e) Change in the corresponding absorbance in-situ FT-IR spectra of am-N, S-CDs.

Femtosecond transient absorption spectra of (a, b) N, S-CDs. (d, e) am-N, S-CDs-500. (g, h) am-N, S-CDs-3500. Time profiles of normalized transient absorption spectra for (c) N, S-CDs, (f) am-N, S-CDs-500 and (i) am-N, S-CDs-3500. (j) Schematic diagram of the amino modification in am-N, S-CDs during the photocatalytic H2O2 production.

head.ce:title: Boosted photocatalytic H2O2 production in pure water with amino-modified N, S-doped carbon dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple hydrothermal method was adopted to modify N, S-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) with feathers as precursors and ammonia by amino modification. The as-prepared am-N, S-CDs in amino as in alkaline environments possessed shorter S-N bonds (S2-), which kinetically and effectively increased the rate of electron conduction and reduced the binding of electron-hole pairs. In addition, the amino modification in am-N, S-CDs effectively improved the O2 adsorption capacity, photocatalytic activity and cycling capacity of the catalysts, with the H2O2 yield (384.86 μM) being 3.73 times higher than that of the pristine N, S-CDs (103.12 μM). This study suggests a rational design strategy for chemical bonding to regulate the surface charge transfer of photocatalysts for photochemical-to-chemical energy conversion.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is a widely used oxidant and multifunctional energy carrier in textile, pharmaceutical, paper, chemical, and environmental protection industries; however, more than 95 % of the H2O2 currently on the market is produced by the anthraquinone process, which consumes a large amount of energy resources, creates toxic by-products, and relies heavily on rare palladium-based catalysts [1–3]. In contrast, artificial photosynthesis of H2O2 using semiconductors is a sustainable and environmentally friendly advanced method, and many reports to date have claimed that H2O2 can be produced using water, oxygen, sunlight, and photocatalysts [4].

As non-metallic and environmentally friendly nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) with the size of < 10 nm, exhibit great potential for applications such as photocatalysts in the field of energy and the environment [5–10]. Most of the known CDs reported in the literature are characterized by a graphite-like core organized into a complex sp2/sp3 carbon network and surrounding shells, depending on the type of organic precursor used in the CDs synthetic assembly process [11–15]. In addition, CDs as a photocatalyst have their own unique advantages that other catalysts are unable to provide as follows: (i) most of the CDs materials are soluble in water, and the reaction system takes place in the catalyst homogeneous phase, with a large contact area between the reaction material and the catalyst that is unprecedented for other powder catalysts [16–18]; (ii) the raw materials for CDs catalysts come from a wide range of sources, and most materials in the natural ecosystem can be used as precursor carbon sources with low-cost for CD synthesis [19–22]; (iii) the incorporation of a large number of CDs cannot block each other to reduce the light-exposed area as in the case of other photocatalysts. Because of this, the utilization of CDs as photocatalyst for photocatalytic H2O2 production is the sensational choice for researchers. In a previous report, we hydrothermally synthesized N, S-doped CDs using feathers from shuttlecock as precursor for greatly enhanced the activity of the photocatalytic reaction by using the S-O group formed upon disulfide bond breaking as the active site [23–27]. Unfortunately, the unstable activity of the S-O moiety resulted in poor cycling efficiency of the photocatalyst and a large gap between the photocatalytic activity and that of the metal-based photocatalysts, and thus a modification strategy is needed to solve the aforementioned problems.

In this work, N, S-CDs were further modified by amino-modified using a simple hydrothermal method with feathers and ammonia as precursors. The prepared amino-modified N, S-CDs (am-N, S-CDs) present short S-N bonds (S2-) in alkaline environments, which is kinetically effective in increasing the migration of electrons rate and reducing the electron-hole pairs’ recombination. In addition, the amino modification in am-N, S-CDs effectively enhanced the O2 adsorption capacity, photocatalytic performance and cycling ability, which showed a 3.73-fold higher H2O2 yield (384.86 μM) than that of the pristine N, S-CDs (103.12 μM). This study presents a rational chemical bond design strategy to regulate the CDs photocatalyst surface charge transfer for photochemical-chemical energy conversion.

Consistent with our reported hydrothermal strategy for preparing N, S-CDs [23], only the ammonia was introduced during the synthesis of am-N, S-CDs. Typically, 0.28 g of feathers, 30 mL of deionized water and a certain amount of ammonia (100, 200, 300, 400, 500, 1000, 2000, 3000, 3200, 3500, 3700, 4000 and 10000 μL) were mixed and added into the Teflon reactor and heated at 200 ℃ for 14 h. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting solution was filtered with filter paper until the large particulate material from the solution was completely removed, giving a yellow transparent liquid as the am-N, S-CDs solution. Finally, the above am-N, S-CDs solution was freeze-dried and named as am-N, S-CDs-x (x = 100, 200, 300, 400, 500, 1000, 2000, 3000, 3200, 3500, 3700, 4000 and 10000 μL). Unless otherwise noted, comparisons in the text are between am-N, S-CDs-500 and am-N, S-CDs-3500 (am-N, S-CDs) and the original sample (am-N, S-CDs).
H2O2 yield test, SCC calculation, H2O2 decomposition test, cycle stability test, electron transfer number and H2O2 selectivity specific steps in this work are given in the Supporting Information .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microstructure and chemical compositions of am-N, S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation process is the hydrothermal method, with only the feathers as the chemical precursor, and the sample is synthesized in an ammonia-rich environment (Fig. 1 a, See the Material synthesis section in the Supporting Information for specific synthesis steps). In general, keratin, the main component of feathers, has abundant disulfide bonds, which can be break within hydrothermal condition and combine with oxygen to form N, S-doped CDs, while under ammonia-enriched conditions, the increased temperature can promote the decomposition of ammonia water into ammonia gas and water, thus ammonia can replace oxygen binding to disulfide bonds’ decomposition products, forming a new surface moiety, NH2 groups, on the surface of N, S-CDs (named as amino modified N, S- CDs, am-N, S-CDs). Next, the morphology of the prepared am-N, S-CDs was subsequently characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). In Fig. 1b, the am-N, S-CDs have an approximate spherical morphology, and the size of them is around 2–4.5 nm, with most sizes being 3–3.5 nm (inset of Fig. 1b), which indicates that the preparation process was controlled quite appropriately and the size of the particles is in the nanometer scale. After compared with the unmodified amino N, S-CDs, the size was not changed significantly (Fig.S1). Furthermore, the lattice streaks with lattice spacing of 0.21 nm can be clearly seen from the high magnification TEM (HRTEM) image, which was ascribed to (100) lattice plane of carbon [28], suggesting the observed am-N, S-CDs have a crystalline structure similar to that of graphite, and this regular lattice spacing shows a high degree of crystallinity. To further investigate the crystal characteristics of am-N, S-CDs, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) technique was carried out. In Fig.S2, it can be seen that a sharp peak appearing at around 21° can be found in both N, S-CDs and am-N, S-CDs, corresponding to the (120) crystal surface of the carbon [29], which suggests that amino modification has not been changed the crystal structure of the carbon dots. The specific functional groups of the modified N, S-CDs were intensively characterized using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). In Fig. 1c, the peaks at 1120 and 1400 cm−1 correspond to the vibrations of the C-S and pyrrole N forms of the C-N bond [30], and the characteristic peak at 3130 cm−1 belongs to the N–H bond, which can tentatively explain the appearance of the pyrrole N peak [31]. In addition, the characteristic absorption bands at 1660 and 3430 cm−1 are attributed to the hydrophilic groups of C = O and O–H/–COOH bonds [32]. Notably, the am-N, S-CDs exhibited a new characteristic peak, which belonged to the N–H bending vibration peak at 1507 cm−1 [33], confirming the successful modification of amino group on the surface of N, S-CDs. UV–vis absorption spectra in Fig. 1d revealed that after amino modification, there was no broadening of the absorption edge over am-N, S-CDs, but the absorption intensity is significantly enhanced, especially in the ultraviolet range, which facilitates the efficient use of high frequency photons. The surface chemistry of the am-N, S-CDs, including the chemical environment and detailed electronic structure of the elements, was determined using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Four elements namely C, N, O, and S can be observed in the XPS survey spectrum of am-N, S-CDs, as illustrated in Fig.S2. Among them, four peaks can be classified in the C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1e) at 287.76, 285.98, 284.82, and 284.15 eV, which can be assigned to C = N/C = O, C-O, C-N, and C–C [34]. In the N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1f), a peak of 161.3 eV appears, attributed to the −amino (molecular) group [[35]], which effectively proves that the amino group was successfully modified on the surface of the N, S-CDs. Finally, in the S 2p spectrum (Fig. 1g), in addition to the thiophene-s and sulphate/sulphonate formed by disulfide bond breakage [36], a disulfide form of the N-S-N bond (S2-) appeared, thus it can be inferred that the amino group is present as ammonium disulfide on the surface of the am-N, S-CDs.

N, S-CDs synthesized from feathers have been shown to be useful for photocatalytic production of H2O2 in our previous work, but its activity and photochemical energy conversion efficiency remained low. In this study, the adjustment of the surface groups of N, S-CDs by amino modification was used to overcome the above-mentioned drawbacks for more efficient and stable photocatalytic production of H2O2 (If not specified, am-N, S-CDs refer to am-N, S-CDs-3500 sample.). Combined with UV–vis spectra (Fig. 1d), Tacu equation curves (Fig.S4) and Mott-Schottky plots (Fig.S5), the energy band positions of am-N, S-CDs can be determined, and the conduction band (CB) potentials of all the samples are negative when compared to the redox potentials of the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) and the oxidation–reduction potentials, suggesting thermodynamic feasibility of generating H2O2 via the ORR pathway (Fig.S6) [37]. The photocatalytic H2O2 produced by the photocatalysts was investigated under simulated sunlight (Fig. 2 a, and the standard curve of H2O2 is presented in Fig.S7), and the modification of the amino group effectively increased the photocatalytic activity of N, S-CDs. Then effect of the volume of added ammonia during the synthesis on the activity of am-N, S-CDs was then explored, the am-N, S-CDs photocatalytic catalysis of H2O2 activity increased significantly with the increase in the volume of added ammonia, with am-N, S-CDs-3500 exhibiting the highest H2O2 evolution rate of 384.86 μM, which is approximately 3.73 times higher than that of the N, S-CDs (Fig. 2b). In addition, the modified am-N, S-CDs showed a significant improvement in solar-to-chemical conversion (SCC) efficiency compared to N, S-CDs. In pure water, the SCC efficiency of am-N, S-CDs for H2O2 production was as high as 0.05 %, which is 5.2 times higher than that of pristine N, S-CDs (Fig. 2c). The generation of H2O2 requires O2 to combine with H+, and the concentration of H+ in the system greatly affects the rate of H2O2 generation. By adjusting the pH of the solution (Fig.S8), the results reveal that pH = 1 is the most favorable for the generation of H2O2, and it also illustrates that the water oxidation pathway (WOR) has an extremely low effect on the am-N, S-CDs photocatalyst. In addition, the amino group had some positive effects on the cycling ability of the photocatalysts, and the performance of the amine-modified am-N, S-CDs, although also degraded after the first cycle, decreased less than that of the N, S-CDs to only 46.7 % after the first cycle (Fig.S9). Fig. 2f summarizes the performance comparison of am-N, S-CDs with the recently reported photocatalytic H2O2 generation from carbon dots and some carbon nitride materials (g-C3N4), with the am-N, S-CDs being far superior to most of the carbon-based catalyst materials reported to date (Table S1). Finally, the specific pathway of H2O2 production from this catalyst was explored, firstly with the addition of a capture agent to the production system, as indicated in Fig. 2g, where the yield H2O2 production rate increases in O2 atmosphere and decreases significantly in N2 atmosphere, suggesting that ORR with external O2 as feedstock is the main pathway. Similarly, the production of H2O2 was severely inhibited under nitrogen atmosphere or when an electron scavenger (AgNO3) was added, suggesting that H2O2 cannot be generated via direct or indirect 2 e- WOR. Therefore, when holes’ scavenger (ethanol EtOH) was added, there was a low decrease in H2O2 production, indicating that the amino modification has not been induced holes to function but rather to enhance the photocatalytic activity of the am-N, S-CDs by increasing the electron utilization. The above results suggest that the ORR half-reaction to produce H2O2 proceeds via a two-step single-electron indirect reduction pathway for the am-N, S-CDs-based photocatalytic system. In order to further understand the oxygen reduction mechanism, the selectivity of H2O2 generation was investigated by using a rotating ring disc electrode (RRDE) to detect H2O2 generation in oxygen-saturated 0.1 M potassium hydroxide electrolyte, and the linear scanning voltametric curves of the obtained samples at 1600 rpm are demonstrated in Fig.S10. The disk currents from the reduction reaction of oxygen were gradually increased in both the am-N, S-CDs and the N, S-CDs, and simultaneously, the disk electrode in which H2O2 produced can rapidly diffuse to the ring electrode and further oxidized to form the oxidation current. The ring current from the oxidation of H2O2 is significantly higher for am-N, S-CDs than that of N, S-CDs, indicating that more H2O2 is produced by am-N, S-CDs. The calculated average number of transferred electrons (n) and H2O2 selectivity (Fig. 2h) of am-N, S-CDs were closer to 2 and its H2O2 selectivity was higher under the same conditions, so the tested results fully proved that the amino modification promotes more electrons for the combination with oxygen to produce more H2O2, which effectively promotes the photocatalytic reaction process on the surface. Meanwhile, electron spin resonance (ESR) was employed to verify the generation of •O2 – intermediates during the ORR reaction process, as demonstrated in Fig. 2i. As can be seen, the N, S-CDs displayed only weak addition signals after 10 min of irradiation, whereas the DMPO-•O2 – signals of am-N, S-CDs were much higher, indicating that the modified catalyst activities and yields were much stronger than those of the pristine N, S-CDs, and also confirming intuitively the two-step single-electron ORR reaction pattern over am-N, S-CDs photocatalytic system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transition of S-N bond in am-N, S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: For investing the reason for enhanced photocatalytic H2O2 activity of amino modification in N, S-CDs, different alkaline raw materials were added during the synthesis of N, S-CDs for testing H2O2 production performance. The two components in ammonia, amine and hydroxide, were compared and it appeared that even with the addition of the same amine or hydroxyl, the corresponding H2O2 production performance was not as high as the activity of that of am-N, S-CDs after the amino-modification (Fig. 3 a). It can be shown that the addition of hydroxyl alone in the synthesis even greatly inhibited the production of H2O2, but the activity of amino modification alone was also lower than that of am-N, S-CDs with the addition of ammonia in the synthetic engineering, which indicates that the amino modification provided by the ammonia water environment can effectively enhance the catalytic activity, while the appropriate alkaline environment has a certain synergistic effect on the amino modification. To further investigate the synergistic effect of alkaline environment on amino modification, am-N, S-CDs-500 and am-N, S-CDs-3500 with different volumes of ammonia added were analyzed by XPS. In Fig. 3b, the high-resolution S 2p XPS spectra of am-N, S-CDs-500 and am-N, S-CDs-3500 exhibits the new peak area of S-N bonds are different, in while the S-N (formed by breaking the disulfide bond and attaching it to the amino group) bond of am-N, S-CDs-3500 is longer than that of am-N, S-CDs-500 [38], which facilitates the transmission of electrons, enhancing the photocatalytic activity. Additionally, temperature-programmed O2 (TPD-O2) desorption indicate that am-N, S-CDs have stronger O2 adsorption capacity and stronger O2 surface binding strength than pristine N, S-CDs (Fig. 3c), which also accounts for the enhanced amine modification from an important point of view in the H2O2 photocatalytic synthesis. In order to further understand the reaction process and real-time intermediates in the photocatalytic H2O2 production process, sample of am-N, S-CDs was studied using the in-situ Fourier transform infrared (in-situ FT-IR) spectroscopy. In Fig. 3d and e, the characteristic peaks of intermediates (⋅HOOH, ⋅OOH, and ⋅O2 –) in the photocatalytic H2O2 production process were found in the spectra [39], which indicated that am-N, S-CDs formed H2O2 in a two-step single-electron process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of S-N bonding on photogenerated electron lifetime
ce:section.ce:para: Finally, the enhancement of activity was kinetically explained by measurements of femtosecond transient absorption (fs-TA) spectra, in which the three samples were excited at 718–863 nm, respectively (Fig. 4 a, d and g). According to their energy band structures, and the fs-TA intensity decreased significantly with the prolongation of probe detection time due to the reduction of the active photo-generated charges (Fig. 4b, e and h) [40]. The kinetic decay curves were subsequently recorded and fitted with a double exponential function, and it was observed that the slow decay component T1 (176.25, 217.35 and 451.7 ps) displaying trapped electrons from the CB-trapped state and the recombination component T2 (7.27, 28.42, and15.67 ps) with rapidly excited electrons and holes (Fig. 4c, f and i) [41], suggesting that the amine modification effectively prolongs the electronic lifetime and that the shorter S-N bonding accelerates the electron migration. Based on the above description, the mechanism of photocatalytic production of H2O2 by am-N, S-CDs was obtained (Fig. 4j), in which amino modification binds to sulfur atoms after disulfide bond breaking to form S-N bonds, and the S-N bonds can absorb more oxygen. The formation of shorter S-N bonds in alkaline environments leads to a faster transfer of electrons from the bulk phase to the surface of the am-N, S-CDs to combine with adsorbed oxygen to form H2O2, which reduces the meaningless combining of electrons and holes from the perspective of reducing electron-hole contact.

In summary, a simple hydrothermal method was adopted to modify N, S-CDs with feathers as precursors and ammonia by amino modification. The as-prepared am-N, S-CDs in amino as in alkaline environments possessed shorter S-N bonds (S2-), which kinetically and effectively increased the rate of electron conduction and reduced the binding of electron-hole pairs. In addition, the amino modification in am-N, S-CDs effectively improved the O2 adsorption capacity, photocatalytic activity and cycling capacity of the catalysts, with the H2O2 yield being 3.73 times higher than that of the pristine N, S-CDs. This study suggests a rational design strategy for chemical bonding to regulate the surface charge transfer of photocatalysts for photochemical-to-chemical energy conversion.

Haoyuan Qin: Writing – original draft, Data curation, Conceptualization. Lei Sun: Data curation. Suchang Zou: Data curation. Ang Bian: Data curation. Yanhua Cui: Data curation. Jianhua Hou: Data curation, Formal analysis. Changyu Lu: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Resources. Chunsheng Li: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Resources. Feng Guo: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Weilong Shi: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21906039, 22006057, 62305135 and 21906072), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2023 M743178), Jiangsu Province Industry-University-Research Cooperation Project (BY20231482) and the Open Fund of the Key Laboratory of Solar Cell electrode Materials in China Petroleum and Chemical Industry (2024A093), Outstanding Youth Project of Hebei GEO University in 2024 (JQ202403), PhD Research Startup Foundation of Hebei GEO University in 2024 (BQ2024026).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2024.156239.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An organic perspective on photocatalytic production of hydrogen peroxide
Nat. Catal.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Freese, J.T. Meijer, B.L. Feringa, S.B. Beil, An organic perspective on photocatalytic production of hydrogen peroxide, Nat Catal 6 (2023) 553-558. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41929-023-00980-x.
